result:
I keep thinking about the day I found the first crack in the air.  
It was late October, the sky the color of tarnished pewter, and I was walking home along the river that is less a river now and more a scar of water sluggish with shopping carts and algae. Nothing about the hour was remarkable except the silence stitched inside it, as though someone had turned the dial on the entire city’s volume way down. My footsteps sounded muffled, intimate, like I was both perpetrator and eavesdropper on my own sound. Then I saw it: a fracture suspended at shoulder height, thin as a ribbon of raw spider silk yet catching the light as if the light itself were being filleted. I reached out, half in disbelief, half convinced it was a trick of the eye—maybe the smear of a migraine. My fingertip met resistance, cool and glass-smooth. The air had become solid there, but brittle, like the thinnest pane of sugar. I pressed harder. The fracture neither widened nor protested; it simply received my touch, the way the morning mouth of a cat receives the first drop of milk. And in that second I understood that geography had quietly shifted its rules: the world had grown frangible.

Nobody else noticed. Commuters swept past: a mother muttering the rosary of her to-do list, two teens sketching the air with their phones as if conducting invisible orchestras, an old man listing under the weight of two grocery bags whose physics had plainly given up. They all walked through the diagonal shaft between river and suburb as if it were still the same corridor it had been yesterday. For them it was. They hadn’t been invited into the new architecture. I stood there a long while, palm against the fissure, feeling the minute itch of autumn gnats against my wrist, breathing the smell of moldering leaves that had begun to resemble the ecclesiastical tang of incense. I didn’t think to photograph it; the phone stayed in my pocket, suddenly infantile, incapable of registering anything more numinous than takeout menus targeted to my browsing history. Instead I leaned in and peered through the crack.

Inside was a corridor, subtly off. Same angle to the river, same constellation of lamp posts, but the leaves there were midsummer-green, luminous as if every chloroplast had swallowed a star. The pavement’s cracks breathed out a thin steam. A single heron stood in the water, startlingly white, staring at me with eyes the metallic blue of butane flame. Music drifted over—the warble of a glass harmonica, perhaps, or something between wind chime and distant cathedral bell. I felt the pull, not magnetic exactly; it was subtler, like leaning into the ambiguous space between one word and the next in prayer. My chest became a cathedral at vespers, perfumed with cedar and frankincense from an interior I didn’t know I owned. I didn’t step through. Cowardice isn’t always fear; sometimes it’s etiquette, the unwillingness to invade whatever house the universe is building next door. So I tore my gaze away and walked on, half expecting the lullaby of sirens and diaspora of birds to resume. They didn’t. The quiet stayed, thick as felt, and when I turned back, the crack had vanished.

But the damage had been done. That night I dreamed I carried a suitcase of river water. Everywhere I tried to set it down the soil puckered away as though offended. I woke at 3:17, throat full of pebbles. Outside, streetlights buzzed like insects under glass, and the darkness looked newly rational. In cartoons trouble arrives with sound track and trumpet; in reality it slides between frames unnoticed, edits continuity while you remember wrong—was there a lamppost there yesterday? didn’t the hallway turn left not right? The mirror gave me back a face that seemed already angled into tomorrow, older, but not by the usual arithmetic, rather by tributaries of somewhere else. I made tea I couldn’t taste. My apartment smelled faintly of wet stone.

Days passed like raffle tickets being torn in half. I began to inventory every impossible second. On the bus a woman wearing two wristwatches (one stopped, one subtracting units whose names taste like berries) slid into the seat behind me and whispered coordinates to my shoulder blade. The driver’s reflection in the windshield belonged to a boy not old enough to shave. When I surfaced at the shopping plaza an entire floor had evaporated: what had been bakery, game arcade, and pharmacy was now the foyer of a decrepit hotel where bellboys in moth-eaten uniforms stood pinioned under chandeliers of salt. I walked among them, touched a bell-shaped chrysalis dangling from a doorknob; it pulsed, warm, as if incubating a small moon. No one challenged me, though all eyes tracked the periphery: we were all refugees at our own check-in.

I started collecting these spontaneous lacunae. Firstly as a scientist, pocket notebook, terse entries: “9:42am traitorous escalator nosedives into turquoise void,” “4:03pm city fountain now emits audible vowel sounds,” “6:11pm neighbor’s dog recites Bashō in voice of my late father.” After the fifth impossible glitch, I abandoned taxonomy. It was like trying to staple mist into file folders. Instead I became curator of astonishment, devoted to peripheral vision. I discovered cracks blooming everywhere: in bus shelters, across library marble, even once along the ridge of a stranger’s clavicle when her blouse slipped at the crosswalk. Most reopened into variants of that first corridor—river rewound or speeded up, heron different hues—though occasionally I glimpsed stranger lands: a desert where clocks grew like cacti; suburb whose lawns were the mild white of a struck match just after flame; a theater lobby with floor to ceiling mirrors reflecting grimy laundromats instead of patrons. Always the same tug, censer-sweet. Always I hesitated, traveler with unread visa, until the seams knitted shut again.

One evening snow began falling upward. Flakes lifted from the ground toward an iron lid of sky, dazzling, like inverted cathode sparks. I stood in the parking lot of the supermarket feeling a hush so absolute it resembled joy. Shoppers froze midway between car and curb, each face lit with recognition, like actors hearing their cue line offstage. I understood that crack or no crack, we were already all in the corridor together, we just hadn’t yet decided how to acknowledge the furniture was moving. A child nearby laughed, soundless, particles of laughter rising like albino moths. And without plan my hand rose to shoulder height and snapped forward—no crack visible—but I felt the air part and freshen, breathe mint and thunder. I stepped. This time the seam stayed open. Behind me the upward snow continued its pilgrimage; ahead, the leaves were summer-green, star-cored. The heron waited. This time I too entered the water—transparent, ankle-deep, warm as veins—and walked until the city drowned itself politely into reed and silence. The crack sealed, but not with violence; it closed the way eyes close when what you behold no longer belongs to distance.

Now I live in two kinds of weather: one everyone’s and one not yet. The river runs whichever direction it chooses; yesterday it spiraled and became the spine of a luminous narwhal breaching through dusk. The trees rehearse copper and green simultaneously—no autumn or spring, only rehearsals of both. Sometimes I see others who crossed: the child who laughed, now wearing a coat sewn from the mall’s escalator steps; the pastry lady who wields a rolling pin shaped like a crescent moon. We trade small gifts: a sparrow half-inscribed with constellations, a coin warm as an egg. We speak sparingly; language here liquefies if lingered on. When we sleep we dream the old city, streets cracked open like pomegranate rind, commuters pausing in brief awe at pockets of sudden sky. In those dreams we look younger—versions who never walked through. Sometimes I reach toward them, wanting to tell them of snow that falls upward, of clocks swelling on desert tongues, yet I always stop. Revelation must discover its own sting. Rules are only hourglasses until someone flips them. So I gather moss from a stone warm as cat, plant it on a tongue of bark that will shortly become heron wingbeats, and wait—patience new as Tuesday, antique as breath—for the next tremor to teach me obedience.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
